title: The British Journal of Sociology in the 1970s: continuity and crisis
abstract: In 1977, on the occasion of the forty-third Hobhouse Memorial Lecture presented at the London School of Economics and published in the December issue of the British Journal of Sociology, Daniel Bell noted that the first was given in 1930 by J.A. Hobson on the topic ‘Towards Social Equality’: ‘are there no constants in sociological thought?’Bell (1977: 419) asked, ‘or is it that there is no progress?’ Reviewing the contents of the BJS over the 1970s, Bell's question is both easy and hard to answer. The easy part is the question of constants – articles appearing during this decade engage with long-standing, even foundational, debates in the discipline as well as with more specific skirmishes of the time; they also portend newer concerns that remain critical today. The harder question concerns the matter of progress. Certainly it is possible to note the changes that have taken place within the discipline in the last three to four decades; that it is difficult to say whether these mark any kind of progress probably has most to do with a recent scepticism about the very concept that was not so typical of the 1970s. Bell was not himself concerned with the issue of social equality. His lecture and article were on ‘The Return of the Sacred? The Argument on the Future of Religion’ (‘are there no constants in sociological thought?’ one might echo, ‘or is it that there is no progress?’) The sociology of religion is one of the constants that link the 1970s to current debates in sociology, in terms of both continuity and change. In the 1970s, the greater number of the papers in this field extended the exegetical treatment of Durkheim's and Weber's works on religion, with several also cast in an anthropological understanding of religion in terms of belief-systems, magic, occult or ritual behaviour. A small number of contributions presage the analysis of religion and politics that is so current today, including studies of the Orange Order in Ireland (Roberts 1971), Islam and political behaviour in Malaysia (Kessler 1972), the Nation of Islam in the USA and Rastafarianism in Jamaica (Watson 1973), and race, religiosity and political activism within Britain's Caribbean population (Pearson 1978). Hobson's focus on social equality, meanwhile (and as Bell implies), had not disappeared in the 1970s. This issue is addressed often, and almost wholly in relation to class, stratification and social mobility: there is so far rather little evidence of the disciplinary progress to come in understanding social equality other than in class terms (although see Krausz 1972; Harper, Mills and Parris 1974; Scharf 1977.) The emerging fields of the sociology of race and ethnicity, and the sociology of gender, instead become visible in studies of race relations, racial attitudes and racism (Tunteng 1976; Studlar 1977; Ward 1978); and the gendered study of deviance (Rosenbaum 1975; Smart 1977; Hagan, Simpson and Gillis 1979). There is one striking way of framing the BJS in the 1970s. The first volume of the decade includes a memorial piece on Morris Ginsberg (1889–1970), written by T.H. Marshall and Donald G. Macrae. The final volume carries David Martin's tribute to Talcott Parsons (1902–1979). In between, Marshall (1893–1981) looks back, aged eighty, on his own ‘British Sociological Career’, with further reflection by Macrae, Dahrendorf, and Lipset (1973). These three – Ginsberg, Parsons, Marshall – were central characters in the establishment of sociology as a discipline in Britain and the USA in the twentieth century, including Ginsberg's role as the first president of the British Sociological Association, and Marshall's in founding the British Journal of Sociology. There is a sense, reading through the journal in the 1970s, of a discipline still close to its origins: Weber is easily the single most-discussed figure in these articles, followed by Durkheim. (Marxist perspectives are also significant, but there appears less direct textual engagement with Marx's own works.) At the same time, there is a sense of disciplinary crisis that now seems an essential part of the sociological condition. Alvin Gouldner published The Coming Crisis of Western Sociology in 1970 (see Friedrichs 1972), and debates over the scientific or objective status of the discipline (Bryant 1970; Jary and Phillips 1971), whether there were ‘two sociologies’ or ‘only one’ (Dawe 1970; Fallding 1972), the claims and the novelty of ethnomethodology (Gidlow 1972; Best 1975; Gorman 1975; Gleeson and Erben 1976), run through the decade. Sociology also appears in this period as a discipline concerned with its relations with its neighbours – with history above all, but also with anthropology (Goody 1973; Bauman 1973; Badcock 1975), the psy-disciplines (Archibald 1976; Bocock 1977) and social policy (Platt 1972). Its engagement with contemporary events is evident in articles on attitudes to the Vietnam War (Schreiber 1973, 1976); elections in Chile and South Africa (Zeitlin and Petras 1970; Lever 1972); and social and political movements including civil rights, counter-cultural and student movements (Watson 1973; Salter 1973; Burawoy 1976; Lewis 1976). Certain themes that recur in the 1970s are less prevalent, even almost absent, in sociological discourse today – perhaps most notably, concepts of bureaucracy and ideology no longer feature in the journal's pages in the regular way they did then. A whole sub-discipline – in the sociology of literature – seems to have faded between then and now; while an important current strand of sociology – the study of science and technology – can be seen around the point of its emergence (see especially Cotgrove 1970; Mulkay and Williams 1971). There is, too, a strong feeling of continuity, if not exactly of progress, in the sociology of academic life itself: including pieces on the evaluation of research units and projects (Wiener and Bayley 1971), academic freedom and higher education (Harrison and Weightman 1974), the ‘early-career’ sociologist (Webb 1974), and sex discrimination in universities (Blackstone and Fulton 1975). The two pieces I have selected to stand for the 1970s in the current anniversary issue can be seen as exemplary of the decade, in that both sit within the broad field of historical sociology that is so central to debates in the BJS during this period. Indeed Immanuel Wallerstein's article is taken from a special issue on ‘History and Sociology’ published in September 1976 (Wallerstein 2010 [1976]). Bryan Turner's (2010 [1974]) contribution on ‘Islam, Capitalism and the Weber Thesis’, moreover, touches on several of the Journal's keynotes at this time: history, religion, Weber. Both are concerned, although in quite different ways, with the study of capitalism as a social and economic system. The reason for highlighting these two articles here, however, is not simply that they work as period pieces. Rather it is due to the influence they exerted in unfolding debates within the discipline, and the relevance they continue to have now. Of course the work of both these sociologists has been subject to criticism in the period since the articles appeared, as well as to their own re-thinking and revision. The commentaries that follow in this anniversary issue are expressive of each of these processes: Michael Mann's work since the 1980s stands in critical relation to Wallerstein's; while Bryan Turner offers the lens of self-reflection. The two articles were selected, with difficulty, from a very long short-list, but one aspect of this selective exercise should be highlighted in particular. In a decade during which significant arguments were made for the value of micro-sociology, these two articles stand clearly on the side of the macro. This is not because micro-sociology has proved less important, convincing or enduring – the opposite might even be true. It is rather because these two speak both to the core debates of their time, and to issues that seem crucial to current sociological and public debate: the social formation of religion; and the ways in which capitalist social and economic relations integrate transnational spaces. In this way, they not only provide intellectual snapshots of a decade, but mark out sites of critical argument today.
